video_title: Fit for TV: The Reality of the Biggest Loser | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: Aunque muchos dicen que The Biggest Loser fue un fracaso por los rebotes, nuestro estudio muestra otra historia: solo 2 de 15 ganadores recuperaron su peso inicial o más; el 87% restante mantiene kilos menos que al entrar, algunos entre 30 y 60 kg por debajo incluso años después. Esto demuestra que, más allá de dietas extremas y rutinas difíciles de sostener, el programa sí logró cambios reales y duraderos: personas que llegaron con obesidad y problemas de salud pudieron transformar su cuerpo, hábitos y estilo de vida. Cada kilo perdido y mantenido no es un detalle menor, sino una victoria tangible y un punto de partida hacia una vida más saludable.

comment_text: I was working as an ethical training in Hollywood at the exact same time. I would occasionally see Bob and Gillian around town. I never watched the show more than a few minutes. I knew it was BS. They broke every rule in the book as to ecthical training. During the same period a friend of mine lived in my apartment off Hollywood blvd and I helped him lose weight. He slept on my floor and in 9 months he completely transformed. We began with him walking in a pool, he lifted weights moderately, ate a clean diet, walked around the gym, low impact workouts,  nothing extreme, and we never counted calories. I sent him home 160 lbs lighter than he showed up. I never earned a dime. It wasn't filmed.I would do it all again. These people who were associated with this production, especially the producers, are Hollywood scum. Shame on them.

video_title: Fit for TV: The Reality of the Biggest Loser | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: As someone who has edited many documentaries, this is the most one-sided documentary I've ever seen. There were over 270 contestants on the show, and we're seeing the same 5 people tell you how awful it was. Plus, Joelle, who's own teammate and best friend, isn't in the documentary, and she was upset with Joelle for not giving it her all. Now Joelle blames it on racism. People magazine looked into the 18 winners, and more than half said it was a positive life-saving experience and have kept most of the weight off. But if you watch the documentary, you'd think that it was just evil Bob and Jillian screaming at everyone for an hour a night. There is no balance to the documentary. If People magazine could find people who found it a positive experience, why do we not see even one person on the screen with that message? It's because the producer's goal is to skewer the show and the people who created it. The one guy who is on the show, who shows off how big he is and says, "Clearly, you can see it didn't work for me." Is that the show's fault or the fact that you didn't continue to eat well or exercise? You don't have to exercise like they do on the show to maintain your weight. But apparently it's the show's fault. I have seen others on podcasts who didn't win but were in the finals who have mostly maintained their weight and felt it was a life-changing experience. Why is that not represented in the documentary??? Because the producers had an agenda. Let me point out that I'm not saying that everything the show did was a good idea, and unfortunately, TV producers will always push the envelope for ratings, which is why the doctor should have pushed back more, but let's not make the entire idea evil and 100% bad. There were positives and negatives based on what I've read from former contestants, but the producers of this doc decided to only show the negatives.

comment_text:
The obesity study that showed the slowed down metabolism is from 2016.. Bummer that there is nothing new.. 
The data was: RMR was 704±427 kcal/d below baseline.. That means 

704 ± 427 kcal/d below baseline

"704 kcal/d below baseline" means that the participants’ resting metabolic rate dropped by 704 kilocalories per day compared to their starting level (baseline).

"±427" indicates the standard deviation, meaning there was a lot of variability among participants. Some people's RMR may have dropped by only ~277 kcal/day, while others may have dropped by over 1100 kcal/day.

I would love to know how these extreme difference can be explained?

Conclusion of that study: 

In conclusion, we found that The Biggest Loser participants regained a substantial amount of their lost weight in the 6 years since the competition, but overall were quite successful at long-term weight loss compared to other lifestyle interventions. Despite substantial weight regain, a large persistent metabolic adaptation was detected. Contrary to expectations, the degree of metabolic adaptation at the end of the competition was not associated with weight regain, but those with greater long-term weight loss also had greater ongoing metabolic slowing. Therefore, long term weight loss requires vigilant combat against persistent metabolic adaptation that acts to proportionally counter ongoing efforts to reduce body weight.

comment_text:
In hindsight this show was *extremely* toxic and did not promote reasonable sustainable methods of weight-loss and it was more about shallow entertainment that was often non-constructive. Extreme calorie deficits and exercising 8 hours a day is not good. People should eat solid volumes of good protein, cut out crap, and exercise on a more sustainable routine starting at a safe starting line. Plus, some folks are genetically inclined to be naturally heavier so realistic goals cannot be the same for everyone, some folks will lose some and then hit a plateau and should focus on maintaining. 

My wife and I were each at one point 310 lbs+, she has lost 75 lbs and I have lost 60 lbs gradually sustained over the last 4 years with the goal of losing more and maintaining for life, and we did it with positive real scientific medical guidance and not toxic ideas such as the ones in this show, and we did it without the usage of drugs too (which should be reserved just for folks with genetic conditions). One big thing I recommend is cutting out alcohol.

comment_text:
It used to be my favorite show and I'd watch it every week with my grandmother. But even way back when, what got me was "AVOID ALL FOODS WITH HIGH FRUCTOSE CORN SYRUP", yet promoted Subway which had high fructose corn syrup. It wasn't after it went off the air I started thinking about how nobody who wasn't on that show workouts 8-12 hours/day. "IT'S SO SHAMEFUL YOU ONLY LOST 10 POUNDS THIS WEEK". "Reality" TV isn't reality because the people running it have something to gain from going to massive extremes just for shock value.

It's interesting even the creators themselves (judging from this trailer) are speaking out against it. It makes Biggest Loser being cancelled an official thing. Nobody who doesn't believe in it gets anything out of watching it.

comment_text:
There's a lot of debate around The Biggest Loser and how it portrayed weight loss, but the real crisis that often gets overlooked is the massive and deadly impact of obesity itself. In the U.S., over 42% of adults are classified as obese. That’s not just a cosmetic issue—it’s a public health emergency.

Obesity is linked to over 230 diseases, including heart disease, type 2 diabetes, stroke, and at least 13 types of cancer. It leads to more than 300,000 premature deaths every year in the U.S. alone. On average, it can shorten a person's life by up to 10 years.

And then there’s the cost. Obesity-related healthcare costs in the U.S. are estimated at $173 billion annually. Individuals with obesity spend over $1,800 more per year on medical expenses compared to those at a healthy weight.

We need to stop sugarcoating this issue. It's not about fat-shaming—it's about confronting a preventable epidemic that’s silently killing millions and bankrupting our healthcare system. Prevention, education, and access to healthier food and lifestyles need to be national priorities.

comment_text: Even with all the "horrible" things this documentary will uncover, people will do anything for $250,000. Doesn't matter what they asked them to do.  These types of shows are for entertainment purposes, what you going to have a show that you could win that kind of money, with no dramatics? Hardly.  Was it extreme conditions, sure seems like it, but what do you think it would be eating 10,000 calories, relaxing, swimming by the pool and getting massages?   Don't you get a nice place to stay for a couple of months? Free food?  Yeah 6 hours a day is extreme working out, BUT many people do this in there regular day lives.  I know people who swim, bike, weights and walk daily and they spend about 4-6 hours doing this. I am sure there were packets of disclaimers and waivers that had to sign telling them exactly what they were in for.  Still today people would love to have this opportunity.  I work out 3 hours a day, 2 hours walking and 1 hour weights. I do it to relax and enjoy the sun.

comment_text: As a weight inclusive and non-diet personal trainer & fitness instructor- this show perpetuated fatphobia in our culture. Once people begin to understand the mental harm that shame can cause, they’re faced with the challenge of rethinking how they approach health as a whole. The way that I train folks is by assuring them that I care about their mental/social and emotional health just as much as they physical. If not more. I tell them that they- (that WE)- are so much MORE than a body and I celebrate strength gains, flexibility increases, an improved self awareness approach AND REST- all without taking their weight, photos or measurements.

video_title: Fit for TV: The Reality of the Biggest Loser | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Okay, soooo they took people  who were obese and had admittedly a lot of addiction problems with food and mental health issues and then they put them on a show for everyone in America and beyond to see as they essentially tortured them down to a "satisfactory" weight by use of excessively unhealthy amounts of exercise and a chef making all their meals, and then some got eliminated for not  meeting their goals, then they dumped them back into the same exact situations they left with no mental health care, no NORMAL trainer, no help from a dietician or anything and expected that 1 year, let alone several years down the road, they'd be able to maintain it? How? 

There is a reason we don't drive an alcoholic to the bar after rehab. They never really seriously addressed any of the issues that got these people to the weights they were, and then when they had to go back to work, pick up the kids from soccer, deal with stressful life situations, there was no kind of support which lead to a lot of them feeling and being shamed for gaining the weight back, some of them, all of it back and then some. This is what nasty ignorant people on the internet think would be the effective solution for fat people who make videos of them normally working out and slowly losing the weight--they always think they need to do 10x more, but weight loss is a process. You didn't gain 100+ pounds over night so why should there be any expectation to lose that much that quickly. No health book, gym manual, nothing recommends that as a strategy for successful long term weight loss.
